mappings.rationaleAI: The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (1569-1795) covers the entire arc of Poland-Lithuania from the Golden Age through the elective monarchy, the Cossack revolt, and the three partitions by Russia, Prussia, and Austria, directly matching all subpaths of A.1.

The Safavid Dynasty (1501-1736) established Shi'ite Islam as the state religion, including Shah Abbas I's restoration of the state and relations with European powers, directly matching B.2.a on Iran from c. 1500 to 1779 and B.2.a.i on the rise of the Safavid dynasty.

Catherine the Great's westernization of Russia, partitions of Poland, wars against the Ottoman Empire, Pugachev Rebellion, and Enlightenment ideals directly match A.3.d on further westernization under Catherine II and A.3.e on the impact of the Enlightenment.

The Siege of Vienna in 1683 and the relief by Jan III Sobieski represent the turning point in Ottoman-European relations and the beginning of Ottoman territorial decline, directly matching B.1.b.i on Ottoman conflicts with Austrians and Poles and A.1.c on Sobieski's victories over the Turks.

The Battle of Lepanto (1571) was fought during Suleyman's successors' era and represents Ottoman expansion in the Mediterranean against a Christian coalition, matching B.1.a.i on the Ottoman Empire as the dominant power and its conflicts.

The emancipation of the serfs in Russia in 1861 under Alexander II, while post-1796, provides essential context for understanding the Russian Empire's social structure. However, this section ends at c. 1796. Reject -- belongs in section 963.

Maria Theresa's first partition of Poland (1772) directly relates to A.1.d.ii on the partitions of Poland, though the primary coverage is Austria's role in broader European affairs covered in section 961.
